title: Pinch-pot bird figurine in air-dry clay
subtitle: A small sculpted bird built from two joined pinched halves
excerpt: A small bird figurine pinched from a single ball of clay, with a separate pinched head joined to the body. The form is closed and hollow, which keeps the wall thin enough to dry without cracking. Air-dry clay sets at room temperature, no kiln required, and the finished bird is decorative only.
sourceNotes: Closed-form pinch construction is a long-standing hand-building approach found in Neolithic figurine traditions across Europe and Asia. V&A and Wikimedia Commons open-access archaeological photography for the historical-form reference. Technique derived from first principles.

glossaryTerms.definition: The drying stage where clay holds its shape but is still damp enough to trim, score, and join. It feels firm like thick leather.
Air-dried but unfired. The clay is pale and uniform in colour. For air-dry clay this is the finished structural state.
Clay diluted with water to a thick brushable consistency. Used as the bonding paste for joining two pieces of clay.

content.text: A small bird is one of the friendliest projects in the pinch-pot family. The body is two pinched halves joined into a hollow egg shape; the head is a smaller pinched cup joined on top. The hollow construction is what makes it work without a kiln: a solid lump of clay this thick would crack as the outside dried faster than the centre. Air-dry clay sets at room temperature over two to three days and the finished bird is decorative.

items.name: Air-dry clay
items.substitution: Paper clay air-dry gives stronger joins

content.text: Preparation

Wedge 200 g of clay on the board for two or three minutes. Divide into two portions: 150 g for the body and 50 g for the head. Roll each into a smooth ball. The body ball should be roughly the size of a small plum; the head ball half that size.
Forming the body
1. Pinch the first half

Slice the 150 g ball in half with a fettling knife or a length of taut thread. Each half becomes one side of the body. Take the first half and shape it into a small ball again, then press the thumb in from the cut side to start a pinch pot. Walk the pinches around the rim a quarter-turn at a time, keeping the wall to 6 mm thickness throughout. The finished half is a small cup roughly 5 cm across the opening.
2. Pinch the second half

Repeat on the second half. Aim for the two cups to match: same diameter at the rim, same wall thickness, same depth. Set them rim-to-rim on the board and check the join meets all the way around. If one rim is wider, gently squeeze it inward until they match.
3. Join the two halves
Score both rims with a needle tool: a cross-hatch of shallow scratches, around 1 mm deep. Brush a thin layer of
slip

(clay watered down to thick cream consistency, made from a small offcut) onto both scored rims. Press the two halves rim-to-rim and rotate slightly to seat the join. A small bead of clay will squeeze out: smooth it around the seam with a damp finger, then with the wooden rib.
4. Shape the body

The closed form is now a hollow egg, around 7 cm long. Roll it gently between the palms to round it. Pinch one end into a slight point to suggest the tail. Press the opposite end inward and upward to make a small flattened platform where the head will sit. Press a small needle hole through the wall at the underside (around 2 mm wide) to let air escape as the clay dries: without it, the trapped air can split the form.
Forming the head

Take the 50 g ball and pinch it into a small hollow cup, walls around 5 mm thick. Pinch the cup closed at the rim to form a hollow ball roughly 3 cm across, then smooth the seam with the rib. Pinch one side outward into a small beak shape, around 8 mm long. Score and slip the base of the head and the platform on the body, then press the head onto the body and rotate slightly. Smooth the join with a damp finger.
Detail

Press two small dots into the head with the blunt end of the needle tool for eyes. Score a few feather lines along the back and tail with the needle tool point: short marks, evenly spaced, suggesting plumage. Smooth the whole bird with a barely damp sponge to lift any clay dust and soften the tooled lines.
Drying
Cover the bird loosely with a single layer of plastic for the first six hours. Slow even drying is what stops the join cracking. Remove the plastic once the surface feels
leather-hard
(firm but still slightly cool to the touch). Turn the bird onto a different face every twelve hours so all sides dry at the same rate. Full drying to
bone-dry
takes two to three days depending on room humidity.
Finishing

Once bone-dry, sand any sharp edges with 220-grit sandpaper outdoors or in a well-ventilated space. Wear a mask: dry clay dust is irritating to breathe. Paint with acrylics or leave plain, then seal with two thin coats of PVA or acrylic varnish brushed on cleanly. The seal protects the surface from handling.
Care
Air-dry clay is not waterproof. Keep the bird indoors and away from damp surfaces. The sealed figurine handles dusting with a soft cloth. Do not wash under running water.
Variations
Paper clay air-dry gives stronger joins and a slightly more forgiving surface for the head-to-body seam. Same method.
Add tiny pinched wings: roll two small flat teardrops, score and slip onto the body sides before the body sets to leather-hard.
Make a small family of three birds in different sizes: scale the body weight to 100 g, 200 g, and 300 g while keeping the wall thickness at 6 mm.

items.symptom: Body splits along the seam during drying
items.cause: Score-and-slip join was too dry or too shallow
items.fix: Score deeper next time (around 1 mm) and use a thicker slip. To rescue, press fresh slip into the crack and dry under plastic for twelve hours

items.symptom: Body bulges or pops during drying
items.cause: Air-release hole missing or too small; trapped air expanded as the surface set
items.fix: Always pierce a 2 mm hole through the wall at the underside before the form sets to leather-hard

items.symptom: Head falls off after a few days
items.cause: Slip not used at the head-to-body join, or surfaces unscored
items.fix: Re-attach with fresh slip and a deeper score; if bone-dry, soak both surfaces with water for a minute before scoring and slipping

items.symptom: Wall too thin and one side caves in
items.cause: Pinching went too quickly without checking thickness
items.fix: Pinch in shorter passes around the whole rim; aim for an even 6 mm throughout. A pencil is roughly 6 mm wide for reference
